summary: Jeff Atwood revisits the VB.NET vs C# debate, reviewing Dan Appleman's updated ebook on the topic. He notes that despite both languages compiling to the same .NET runtime, C# developers inexplicably earn more than VB.NET developers for equivalent work. While acknowledging that language choice is largely superficial given the shared framework, Atwood admits he still converts C# code to VB.NET for better comprehension, suggesting the mental overhead of switching languages is real. He concludes that IDE quality matters far more than language choice, as the 'significant issues' in language selection are actually IDE features.
key_points: C# developers are paid more than VB.NET developers despite writing functionally identical .NET code
95-99% of the knowledge needed for VB.NET and C# development is identical due to the shared framework
Converting code to your preferred language isn't about inability to read the other—it's about reducing mental overhead
The 'significant issues' in language choice (background compilation, edit and continue, refactoring) are actually IDE features, not language features
IDE investment yields far greater productivity gains than language selection
money_quotes: C# developers are paid more than VB.Net developers to write the very same .NET framework code.

The existence of separate VB .NET and C# content is largely an indication that publishers, bookstores and conference organizers believe their customers are too stupid to realize the fundamental truth: that it's all about the framework.
I don't convert code because I feel morally obligated to; I do it because I feel like I don't really understand the code unless I've touched every line of it.
The mental overhead of putting on different language hats sure as hell isn't helping us get work done.
The effectiveness of your IDE is more important than any language choice you can make.
The real money play is to invest in the IDE, not the languages.
A kick-ass IDE will have a huge impact on your productivity compared to the utterly meaningless choice between editing VB.NET or C# in Notepad.
key_insight: Language wars distract from what actually matters: your IDE's quality and your comfort with your tools determine productivity far more than syntax preferences.
